did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses a Collective Intelligence approach with AI, specifically employing deep learning models including ResNet, Inception, VGG, and Transformers (ViT-B16, Swin Transformer) to classify melanoma and other skin tumors using datasets like HAM10000 and a dataset from Virgen del Rocío University Hospital.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving the creation of five different configurations using various datasets (HAM10000, HUVR, Stanford) to evaluate a Collective Intelligence approach with AI models (ResNet, Inception, VGG, Transformers) for melanoma detection. It reports specific results, including accuracy metrics (up to 95.3% in Table IV), and discusses limitations like overfitting. Although it includes a literature review section (Section II) using PRISMA methodology to contextualize the study, the primary contribution is the novel experimental validation of the proposed method, classifying it as a research paper.

title: Analysis and Detection of Melanoma through Collective Intelligence with AI
journal: 2024 IEEE 37th International Symposium on Computer-Based Medical Systems (CBMS)
brief_summary: This study investigates the efficacy of a Collective Intelligence approach using deep learning models, including ResNet, Inception, VGG, and Transformers (ViT-B16, Swin Transformer), for classifying melanoma and other skin tumors. The authors created five different configurations to evaluate performance on datasets such as HAM10000, Stanford, and a clinical dataset from Virgen del Rocío University Hospital. The main evaluation approach involved comparing these configurations against each other and assessing their ability to reduce classification errors compared to single-model approaches. Key results indicated that the Collective Intelligence method achieved up to 75.89% accuracy in stress tests on difficult images, with specific configurations showing specialization for different skin tones. However, the study also identified significant overfitting issues, particularly due to a lack of images in certain classes, suggesting a need for data expansion strategies in future work.

lesion_types_studied.details: Class 5 in the complete classification (CF1, CF4, CF5) and grouped under Malignant in binary classification (CF2, CF3). Studied across HAM10000, HUVR, and Stanford datasets.

Referred to as 'Carcinoma' (Class 1) in the HUVR dataset's complete classification (CF1, CF4, CF5) and grouped under Malignant in binary classification (CF2, CF3). The introduction mentions basal cell carcinoma as a type of skin cancer that does not metastasize.

Mentioned in the introduction as a type of skin cancer alongside melanoma and basal cell carcinoma, but not listed as a specific class in the experimental datasets (HUVR, HAM10000, Stanford) used for the models.
Class 6 in the complete classification (CF1, CF4, CF5) and grouped under Benign in binary classification (CF2, CF3). The most abundant class in the HUVR dataset (684 images in CF1).
Class 2 in the complete classification (CF1, CF4, CF5) and grouped under Benign in binary classification (CF2, CF3).
Class 3 in the complete classification (CF1, CF4, CF5) and grouped under Benign in binary classification (CF2, CF3).
Class 4 in the complete classification (CF1, CF4, CF5) and grouped under Benign in binary classification (CF2, CF3). The least abundant class in the HUVR dataset (33 images in CF1).
Class 7 in the complete classification (CF1, CF4, CF5) and grouped under Benign in binary classification (CF2, CF3).
Class 1 in binary classification (CF2, CF3). Composed of Dermatofibroma, Lentigo, Vascular Lesion, Nevus, and Keratosis.
Class 2 in binary classification (CF2, CF3). Composed of Carcinoma and Melanoma.

all_task_types.target: Discrete diagnostic categories of skin tumors (e.g., Melanoma, Basal Cell Carcinoma, Nevus, etc.) and binary labels (Benign vs. Malignant).
all_task_types.details: The paper performs multi-class classification (7 classes: Carcinoma, Dermatofibroma, Lentigo, Vascular Lesion, Melanoma, Nevus, Keratosis) and binary classification (Benign vs. Malignant). The primary task is classifying images into these discrete diagnostic categories using deep learning models (ResNet, Inception, VGG, Transformers) within a Collective Intelligence framework. Evaluation metrics include accuracy, precision, recall, and F1-score.

datasets_used.name: Virgen del Rocío University Hospital (HUVR)
datasets_used.description: A private clinical dataset generated at the Virgen del Rocío University Hospital in Seville, Spain, containing dermoscopic images taken on real patients. It was used to verify the performance of Collective Intelligence with real patient data.

use.training: Used for training in configurations CF1 (complete classification), CF2 (binary classification), CF3 (combined with Stanford for binary), CF4 (combined with HAM10000 for complete), and CF5 (refined CF1).
use.testing: Used for testing in configurations CF1, CF2, CF3, CF4, and CF5. Also used for a stress test with 55 difficult images.

datasets_used.name: HAM10000
datasets_used.description: A public dataset containing 10,015 dermoscopic images with 7 classes, described as one of the most complete public datasets for skin disease.

use.training: Used for training in configuration CF4 (combined with HUVR for complete classification) and CF5 (refined CF1).
use.testing: Used for testing in configurations CF4 and CF5.

datasets_used.name: Diverse Dermatology Images by Stanford University
datasets_used.description: A public dataset containing 656 images representing 570 unique patients, used to address the lack of dark skin tone samples in other datasets.

use.training: Used for training in configuration CF3 (combined with HUVR for binary classification via oversampling).
use.testing: Used for testing in configuration CF3.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To analyze classification performance and irregularities between classes.
To evaluate model performance and compare configurations.
To combine predictions from multiple models in the Collective Intelligence framework by multiplying accuracy percentages with weights and summing column values.
To balance the number of images per class and mitigate overfitting in underrepresented classes.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. The study's primary goal was to verify if a mixture of experts (MoE) approach could classify images more effectively than single models to reduce classification errors, but this was evaluated using descriptive performance metrics (accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score) rather than statistical hypothesis testing.

HUVR (Virgen del Rocío University Hospit.description: Private clinical dataset used for training and testing in configurations CF1, CF2, CF3, CF4, and CF5. Also used for a stress test with 55 difficult images.

stress_test_results.description: Test on 55 difficult images provided by HUVR

HUVR (Virgen del Rocío University Hospit.notes: Results show solid performance but significant overfitting in least populated classes. CF3 is noted for versatility due to training with dark and white skin tones. CF4 demonstrates excellent results in both HAM10000 and HUVR, specializing in white skin tones. CF5 did not achieve expected results in reducing overfitting.

Stanford University Dataset.description: Public dataset containing 656 images of 570 unique patients, used to address lack of dark skin tone samples. Used for training/testing in CF3.

CF3 (Binary Classification).Note: Accuracy is exact in CF3, attributed to training with images of people with dark skin tones.

Stanford University Dataset.notes: Accuracy was not exact in other configurations for this dataset. CF3 offers more versatility as it was trained with cases of both dark and white skin tones.

HAM10000.description: Public dataset containing 10,015 dermoscopic images with 7 classes. Used for training/testing in CF4 and CF5.
HAM10000.notes: Accuracy was not exact in other configurations for this dataset. CF4 demonstrates excellent results in both HAM10000 and HUVR datasets, indicating specialization in handling cases of white skin tones exclusively.

architecture_used.main_model: Collective Intelligence (Mixture of Experts) framework aggregating multiple deep learning models: ViT-B16, Swin Transformer, EfficientNet B0, Xception, ResNet152, DenseNet201, MobileNetV2, and InceptionResNetV2.
architecture_used.pretraining: Pre-trained neural networks (specific models not explicitly detailed as ImageNet or other specific pretraining sources in the provided text, though standard practice for these architectures is implied).

architecture_details.aggregation_method: Weight matrix aggregation based on model accuracy per class. Accuracy percentages of corresponding classes are multiplied with weights of each corresponding class, column values are summed, and the class with the highest sum is chosen.
architecture_details.model_selection_criteria: Only models containing 60% or more accuracy were included in the collective intelligence.

regularization.CF5: Dropout layers added at different levels of all models; lower hyperparameter settings.

training_details.class_imbalance_handling: Oversampling applied to less numerous classes.

classification_or_output_head.CF1_CF4_CF5: Dense layer with 7 neurons and Softmax activation
classification_or_output_head.CF2_CF3: Dense layer with 2 neurons and Softmax activation

preprocessing_techniques: Oversampling applied to balance the number of images per class and mitigate overfitting in underrepresented classes (targeting the highest class count: 684 for CF1/CF3, 1136 for CF2).
Application of dropout layers at different levels of all models to mitigate overfitting (specifically in CF5).
Reduction of learning rates (hyperparameter settings) to mitigate overfitting (specifically in CF5).
Limiting the number of training epochs to mitigate overfitting (specifically in CF5).
Standardization of image capture patterns (focusing on the lesion and surrounding skin in the center) to reduce noise and improve classification consistency.

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Applied to balance the number of images per class and mitigate overfitting in underrepresented classes. Target class counts were set to the highest class count in the respective configuration: 684 for CF1/CF3, 1,136 for CF2, and 1,000 for CF4 (limiting Nevus images to 1,000). Applied carefully to avoid introducing excessive noise.

conclusions: The Collective Intelligence (CI) approach using a mixture of deep learning models (ViT-B16, Swin Transformer, EfficientNet B0, Xception, ResNet152, DenseNet201, MobileNetV2, InceptionResNetV2) demonstrates solid overall performance for classifying melanoma and other skin tumors, achieving up to 95.3% accuracy in multi-class classification (CF1) and 91.51% in binary classification (CF3) on the HUVR dataset.
The CI method achieved up to 75.89% accuracy in stress tests on difficult images, indicating potential for reducing classification errors compared to single-model approaches.

Significant overfitting was observed, particularly in the least populated classes (e.g., Vascular Lesion), which was attributed to the lack of training images and the use of oversampling, leading to poor generalization for those specific classes.

Configuration CF3, which combined the HUVR dataset with the Stanford University dataset (containing dark skin tone images), showed versatility and exact accuracy on the Stanford dataset, suggesting improved handling of diverse skin tones compared to configurations trained exclusively on white skin tones (like CF4).
Configuration CF4 demonstrated excellent results on both the HAM10000 and HUVR datasets, specializing in handling cases of white skin tones exclusively.

The study acknowledges limitations in applying these models to clinical settings due to the need for consistent image capture patterns; specifically, images must focus on the lesion and surrounding skin in the center to avoid noise and ensure correct classification.
Future work should focus on data expansion strategies, specifically adding more diverse and representative images for underrepresented classes, to improve model generalization and performance.
Future research should develop programs to transform input images into the specific format used for training to allow the use of images that do not naturally follow the required capture pattern.
important_remarks: Significant overfitting was observed, particularly in underrepresented classes (e.g., Vascular Lesion with only 33 images), despite the use of oversampling and regularization strategies in configuration CF5.

The study highlights a critical limitation regarding skin tone bias: public datasets like HAM10000 lack sufficient samples of dark skin tones, necessitating the inclusion of the Stanford dataset to improve generalization for diverse populations.

Clinical deployment is constrained by strict image capture requirements; models require images focused on the lesion and surrounding skin in the center, and performance degrades significantly if this pattern is not followed.
The stress test on 55 difficult real-world images yielded lower accuracy (58.62% to 75.89%) compared to standard test sets, indicating potential gaps in robustness for challenging clinical cases.

Configuration CF4, trained on HUVR and HAM10000, showed excellent performance but specialized exclusively in white skin tones, limiting its applicability to diverse demographics without further data expansion.

The paper does not report formal statistical hypothesis testing (p-values or confidence intervals); results are presented using descriptive metrics (accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score) without significance testing.
